result:
The old woman at the farmers market sells honey in mason jars with handwritten labels, and every Saturday she tells me the same story about her bees. How they know her footsteps. How they dance in figure-eights to share the location of wildflower meadows. How once, during a thunderstorm, she found them all huddled together in the hive, humming at a frequency she could feel in her bones.

I don't know if I believe her, but I buy the honey anyway.

There's something about repetition that creates its own kind of truth. The way my father cleared his throat before saying something important. The way cats always find the one patch of sunlight on the floor. The way everyone claims their grandmother made the best version of whatever food they're eating.

Memory is unreliable, they say, but what else do we have? I remember the taste of tap water from garden hoses in summer, metallic and warm and somehow perfect. I remember my first dog's eyes going cloudy, how he still wagged his tail when he heard my voice. I remember the exact shade of blue nail polish my mother wore the day she taught me to parallel park, though I can't remember what we talked about.

Sometimes I make lists of things I'm afraid I'll forget:
- The sound of VHS tapes rewinding
- Phone numbers we used to memorize
- How to fold a paper fortune teller
- The smell of libraries before they all started serving coffee
- Waiting for photos to develop, the anticipation of not knowing

My neighbor grows tomatoes in his backyard. He's eighty-three and waters them every evening at 6:15, wearing the same canvas hat. Last week, he gave me a bag of Cherokee Purples, these ugly, beautiful things that looked almost bruised. "They don't ship well," he said, "so you won't find them in stores." They tasted like summer concentrated, like the platonic ideal of tomato. I ate them with just salt, standing over the sink.

We're losing things all the time. Not just species and glaciers and languages, though yes, those too. We're losing the ability to be bored. To not know something immediately. To be unreachable. My niece doesn't understand why we can't just pause live TV, why some movies aren't available to stream, why she can't video call her friend who's camping.

But we're gaining things too, I suppose. My mother video-tours me through her garden from three thousand miles away. I can hear any song I want, instantly. These words I'm writing could be read by someone in Mumbai or Stockholm or a small town in Kansas I've never heard of.

Still, I miss handwriting. Not romantically, but actually miss it. The way your hand would cramp during essay exams. The specific anxiety of running out of ink. The way you could tell someone's mood by their penmanship. I have a box of letters from an old friend who died five years ago. Her handwriting got smaller as she got sicker, as if she was trying to fit more words onto each page, racing against time.

The honey woman's story changes slightly each week, though she doesn't seem to notice. Sometimes the storm is in June, sometimes August. Sometimes the bees hum in B-flat, sometimes C. But the heart of it remains: the bees knew she would keep them safe, and so they sang.

I've started keeping my own bees, though I haven't told her. They're nothing special—just regular honeybees in a white box in my backyard. I don't know their language yet. They don't recognize my footsteps. But yesterday, I sat near the hive for an hour, just listening. They do hum. It's not musical, exactly, but it's not not musical either. It's the sound of ten thousand small bodies doing the work of living.

There's a certain kind of loneliness that comes from being the only one who remembers something. The last speaker of a language. The only survivor of an accident. The sole keeper of a family recipe. But there's also a kind of power in it, a terrible responsibility. You become the bridge between what was and what is.

My grandmother used to make these cookies—brown butter with cardamom and rose water. I watched her make them dozens of times but never wrote down the recipe. She cooked by feel, by smell, by the way the dough pulled away from her fingers. After she died, I spent years trying to recreate them. I came close once, close enough that I cried into the mixing bowl, but it was never quite right. The flavor was there but not the feeling. The taste but not the time.

Now I make my own version. Different, but related. A descendant rather than a replica. I write down everything: measurements, temperatures, the exact color the butter should turn. Someday someone might want to know.

The honey woman won't be at the farmers market forever. Her hands shake now when she counts change. She's started bringing her daughter to help carry the jars. But for now, every Saturday, she tells me about the storm, about the humming, about the bees who know her footsteps.

And every Saturday, I listen like it's the first time.
